metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or operationalize the 'net process friction does not increase' criterion with a measurable proxy (e.g., 'the number of required flags/prompts on `rebar close` does not increase from N to N+1', verifiable by `rebar close --help` diff against baseline).
evidence: 'Net process friction does not increase' — no definition of friction, no baseline measurement, no threshold
AC4 only verifies the field exists via `jq -e '.close_class'`, not that user effort decreased or stayed constant
No command timing, step-count, or before/after comparison is specified
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Net process friction does not increase' is subjective and has no defined measurement method or baseline, making it unevaluable. None of the four ACs provide a concrete check for this claim beyond AC4's field-existence assertion.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Net process friction does not increase'
scenarios: The new `--class` enum adds a required flag with 10 options; friction objectively increases but the plan has no way to detect it
A reviewer accepts the criterion as met simply because the free-text field was removed, without measuring actual effort
findings.suggested_fix: State the current required-argument count for `rebar close` (e.g., 'currently requires 1 positional arg + 1 `--reason` free-text; after this change requires 1 positional arg + 1 `--class` enum with no free-text') so friction is concretely comparable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that operationally validates 'net process friction does not increase' — e.g., assert that `rebar close --class <enum>` requires no more keystrokes/flags than the old `rebar close --reason <text>`, or document a baseline vs. post-change step-count comparison.
evidence: Success Criteria states: 'Net process friction does not increase.'
AC4 only verifies that `.close_class` is present on a closed bug — it does not measure whether the total steps/time for bug close changed.
No AC references a baseline step-count, timing, or agent-session comparison.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Net process friction does not increase' has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion that observably validates it — there is no before/after comparison, user-study proxy, or measurable baseline to confirm the criterion was met.
findings.location: Success Criteria – 'Net process friction does not increase'
scenarios: The enum replaces free-text but adds a required selection step that wasn't there before, silently violating the criterion with no AC to catch it.
A reviewer or auditor cannot confirm compliance with this success criterion from the acceptance tests alone.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Bug-close command requires ≤ N keystrokes/flags compared to the prior free-text flow, verified by a side-by-side CLI invocation comparison in the test suite or documented in a decision record.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that verifies backfill execution — e.g., assert that `rebar metrics --since <earliest-known-date>` returns at least one metric entry with `source=backfill_classified` (or `source=backfill_structural`) and a valid `confidence` value, proved against the real event store in an integration test.
evidence: Success Criteria: 'Historical data is backfilled wherever a durable source exists; backfilled/classified values are labeled with source+confidence and segregated from authoritative structural signals.'
AC3 tests the segregation contract in a unit test but does NOT verify that the actual backfill was performed or that real historical records carry the correct labels.
The Backfill track in Decomposition is described as 'one-time, labeled' but no AC asserts completion or spot-checks real backfilled records.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Historical data is backfilled wherever a durable source exists; backfilled/classified values are labeled with source+confidence and segregated from authoritative structural signals' has no dedicated Acceptance Criterion to verify the backfill was actually executed and correctly labeled.
findings.location: Success Criteria – final bullet ('Historical data is backfilled…')
scenarios: Backfill scripts are written but never run; AC3 passes because the unit test uses synthetic data, while production data remains unbackfilled.
Backfill is run but labels are omitted on some record types; no AC catches the omission.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an expand-contract sequence (or explicit rollback plan) for the `--class` enum replacement: specify whether `--reason` is deprecated-then-removed or immediately replaced, document the migration path for existing callers, and state how the enum set can be extended without another breaking change.
evidence: "the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded `--class` enum persisted on the STATUS event"
"replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text reason"
"old records stay valid; readers tolerate absence" — this addresses the data layer only, not the CLI call-site breakage
AC4: "the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded `--class` enum" with no rollback or deprecation period described
findings.finding: The bug-close CLI change (replacing the required free-text reason with a `--class` enum) is a breaking interface change for any agent or script that currently passes the free-text argument, but the plan provides no rollback/back-out path or expand-contract sequencing for the CLI surface — it only asserts that old *records* remain valid, which does not address the broken call sites.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; Decomposition — Instrumentation track; Approach paragraph
scenarios: An agent or CI script that currently calls `rebar close <id> --reason "some text"` will fail with an unrecognised argument or missing required enum after the change ships, with no migration path documented.
If the initial `--class` enum set proves inadequate (missing a needed category), there is no stated mechanism to extend or roll back without another breaking change.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a deprecation window: accept both `--reason <text>` (mapped to `class=unclassified`, logged as deprecated) and `--class <enum>` for one release cycle, then remove `--reason`. Document the enum extension path (e.g., additive enum values are non-breaking; removal requires a new deprecation cycle). State the rollback step if the enum set ships incomplete: revert to accepting free-text while a corrected enum is designed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the source vocabulary: either add `backfill_structural` to c085's enumerated source set and `is_authoritative()` allowlist, or change 5062 to emit `source=backfill_classified` (consistent with c085 and 538c).
evidence: 5062 Approach: 'Backfilled classes carry `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`'
c085 Approach: '`source` in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified} and `confidence` in {high, classified}'
`backfill_structural` does not appear in c085's source enumeration
findings.finding: Child 5062 emits `source=backfill_structural` but child c085's authoritative source vocabulary enumerates only `{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}`. The value `backfill_structural` is absent from c085's enum, so `is_authoritative('backfill_structural')` will fall into an undefined/default branch — likely returning False (correct intent) but without an explicit guard, making the segregation guarantee fragile and the schema inconsistent.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61 (Approach section) vs child c085-6d11-d68d-422a (Approach section)
scenarios: The `is_authoritative()` helper in c085 is implemented as an allowlist check; `backfill_structural` is not on the allowlist so it is correctly excluded, but the value string is unrecognised and may trigger a warning or be treated as an unknown source.
A future reader checks `source == 'backfill_classified'` to detect non-authoritative data and misses `backfill_structural` records, causing them to be incorrectly included in authoritative series.
findings.suggested_fix: In c085's registry.py, add `backfill_structural` to the source enum and ensure `is_authoritative('backfill_structural')` returns False explicitly. Update 5062's test to assert the label using the c085-canonical value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and verify the `git blame` derivation algorithm for `caused_by`: specify which file(s)/line(s) to blame (sourced from `file_impact` or another field), which `git blame` flags handle renames (`--follow`) and merge commits (`-M`, `-C`), and what the fallback is when `file_impact` is absent — and add a spike or prototype before committing the full Instrumentation track.
evidence: Plan: 'bug provenance via a new `caused_by` link relation (auto-derived from blame, plus an optional param)'
Plan: 'Instrumentation: the `caused_by` link relation (additive); ... blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation + `--caused-by` param + bug-template advisory'
ABSENCE: `git blame` appears zero times in src/rebar/** (search confirmed no matches)

ABSENCE: no existing rebar code maps a bug ticket to a git commit via blame; the only git-history reader in the close path uses `git log --format=%B%x00` to find referencing commits, not to find causal commits
Unverified evidence class: the git blame subcommand exists but the specific derivation surface (file selection, line selection, merge-commit handling) is unconfirmed
findings.finding: The plan asserts `git blame`-based auto-derivation of `caused_by` links as a non-optional Tier-C instrumentation deliverable, but `git blame` is never used anywhere in the codebase and the specific invocation surface is entirely unspecified: which file(s) and line(s) to blame for a given bug ticket, how to handle multi-file bugs, merge commits, renames (`--follow`), or tickets with no `file_impact`. The capability gap — mapping a bug ticket to a blameworthy commit — is non-trivial and unverified. A naive `git blame` on the first `file_impact` file at HEAD would silently produce wrong provenance links for refactors, rebases, and bugs with no file_impact.
findings.location: Instrumentation track / Decomposition: 'blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation + `--caused-by` param + bug-template advisory'
scenarios: A bug with `file_impact: [{path: 'src/foo.py'}]` — blame at HEAD of that file attributes the last author to touch each line, which for a recently-refactored file is not the commit that introduced the bug.
A bug with no `file_impact` — the derivation algorithm has no input; auto-derivation silently produces no link or a wrong one.
A bug caused by a merge commit — `git blame` skips merge commits by default; the causal commit is missed.
A renamed file — `git blame` without `--follow` attributes lines to the rename commit, not the original author.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `caused_by` auto-derivation via `git blame` targets the **pre-fix state** (e.g. `git blame <parent-of-fix-commit> -- <file>`) so blame identifies the commit that *introduced* the defect, not the one that fixed it; document the exact git invocation in the Instrumentation child spec.
evidence: Plan text: 'bug provenance via a new caused_by link relation (auto-derived from blame, plus an optional param)'
git blame on a file at the fixing commit SHA points to the fixing commit for changed lines, not the introducing commit
No codebase blame invocation exists yet (search for 'git blame' returned no hits in src/rebar)
findings.finding: The `caused_by` auto-derivation via `git blame` is mechanically ambiguous: running blame on the post-fix state attributes lines to the fixing commit, not the introducing commit. To identify what caused the bug, blame must target the pre-fix state (e.g. the parent of the fixing commit, or the file at the commit just before the fix). The plan says 'auto-derived from blame' without specifying which state is blamed, so an implementation that naively blames the current file will systematically return the wrong commit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, Tier-C blindspots bullet: 'bug provenance via a new caused_by link relation (auto-derived from blame, plus an optional param)'
scenarios: An agent closes a bug and the auto-derived caused_by link points to the bug-fix commit itself rather than the commit that introduced the defect, producing a useless or wrong provenance signal.
A user relies on caused_by for trend analysis; the metric shows the fixer as the 'cause', inverting the intended signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: run `git blame <fix_commit>^1 -- <changed_file>` (the parent of the fixing commit) on the lines that the fix deleted/modified, to identify the commit that last wrote the buggy code. The `--caused-by` optional param remains the override for cases where blame is ambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen 9a5a's integration-test jq assertion to `jq -e '[.metrics | keys[]] | length == ($EXPECTED_COUNT | tonumber)'` or compare against the registry's known id set, matching the parent AC1 requirement.
evidence: Parent AC1: 'an integration test asserts via `jq -e` that no registered metric key is absent'
9a5a AC1: '`jq -e '.metrics | keys | length > 0'` succeeds, with every registry id present' — 'with every registry id present' is prose intent, not the jq assertion written
findings.finding: Child 9a5a's integration-test AC asserts only `jq -e '.metrics | keys | length > 0'`, which passes even if registered metric keys are silently absent. The parent AC1 requires that NO registered metric key is absent, verified by `jq -e` — a strictly stronger assertion that 9a5a does not commit to.
findings.location: child 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a, Acceptance Criteria #1
scenarios: A metric reader (e.g. 18e6 or 3c07) fails to register its metrics in c085's REGISTRY; the CLI test still passes because length > 0, but the parent integration test would fail the completeness check.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py`, after `jq -e '.metrics | keys | length > 0'`, add a second assertion that iterates `REGISTRY` ids in Python and asserts each key is present in the parsed JSON output.

evidence: docs/ticket-model.md: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_help.out: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike/pilot step before implementing blame-based auto-derivation: run `git blame` against a sample of closed bugs in the existing history, manually verify attribution against actual fix commits, and define a minimum precision threshold (e.g., ≥80%) that must be met for auto-derivation to be enabled by default rather than opt-in.
evidence: "blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation + `--caused-by` param + bug-template advisory"
No spike, prototype, measurement plan, or success metric for blame accuracy is mentioned anywhere in the plan
The plan provides a manual `--caused-by` override, which implicitly acknowledges blame may be wrong, but provides no measurement of how often that override will be needed
findings.finding: The blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation is an unvalidated heuristic whose accuracy (precision/recall against real bug provenance in this project's history) is asserted but never probed — git blame is known to misattribute in the presence of reformats, renames, and trivial commits, and the plan contains no spike or pilot to measure reliability before full build-out.
findings.location: Decomposition — Instrumentation track ('blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation'); Acceptance Criteria — AC3
scenarios: Blame consistently points to a mass-reformat or dependency-update commit, polluting `caused_by` links across many bugs and making the metric misleading rather than useful.
The auto-derivation is trusted in dashboards without any baseline measurement of accuracy, leading to incorrect trend conclusions about bug provenance.
findings.suggested_fix: Before building the auto-derivation, run a one-day spike: take 20 closed bugs from the existing event store, apply the proposed blame heuristic, and compare results to known fix commits (or manual inspection). If precision is below threshold, make auto-derivation opt-in (`--auto-caused-by` flag) with a disclaimer, and rely on `--caused-by` as the primary path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update 555e's integration test fixtures to include `--class <value>` on every bug-close call to remain compatible with ed13's required --class flag.
evidence: ed13 AC2: 'closing a bug with NO `--class` exits nonzero with a message naming the allowed values'
555e AC2: 'an ambiguous two-commit blame yields no auto `caused_by` link — integration test asserts absence' — the test must close the bug, and the close will require --class
555e AC1 shows `--class regression` in one fixture but the ambiguous-blame fixture (AC2) does not specify --class
findings.finding: Child 555e's integration tests for the ambiguous-blame case (and potentially the auto-derivation case) close bugs via `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed` without necessarily supplying `--class`, but child ed13 makes `--class` REQUIRED on bug close. The 555e tests were authored independently of ed13's requirement and will fail at the transition step if `--class` is omitted.
findings.location: child 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169, Tests section and Acceptance Criteria #2
scenarios: 555e's integration test for the ambiguous-blame path calls `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed` without `--class`; post-ed13 this exits nonzero, causing 555e's own test to fail for the wrong reason.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_caused_by_autoderive.py`, add `--class regression` (or `--class undetermined`) to every `rebar transition ... closed` invocation in the test fixtures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (or a final integration step) as the canonical golden-snapshot regeneration owner; add a note in both 0b96 and 9a5a that the golden snapshots must be regenerated once after BOTH changes are merged.
evidence: 0b96 Files: 'tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate the ~8 golden CLI-help snapshots that enumerate the relation set (e.g. `link__help.out`, `__overview_help.out`, `__overview_dashhelp.out`)'
9a5a Files: 'tests/golden/cli_help/*.out — regenerate the overview snapshots (`__overview_help.out`, `__overview_dashhelp.out`) and add `metrics__help.out`'
Both children list `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` changes and both list `__overview_help.out` regeneration
findings.finding: Children 0b96 and 9a5a both claim to regenerate the same golden CLI-help snapshot files (`tests/golden/cli_help/*.out`, `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt`) with no coordination, creating a scope overlap and ordering conflict on shared artifacts.
findings.location: child 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d (Files section) and child 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (Files section)
scenarios: 0b96 is implemented first and regenerates the golden snapshots with caused_by listed; 9a5a is then implemented and regenerates them again adding metrics — this works but requires a double-regeneration pass.
0b96 and 9a5a are implemented in parallel; both regenerate the snapshots from their own branch state; merging creates a conflict on the snapshot files.
0b96's golden snapshots are accepted in review; 9a5a's regeneration accidentally drops the caused_by addition if the regeneration is run from a state that doesn't include 0b96.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit note in both children that golden CLI-help snapshot regeneration is a final integration step performed after all relation and subcommand additions are complete, and add a CI gate that runs the golden test suite as a post-merge check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'Tier-C' inline in Success Criteria or replace it with the plain-language enumeration already given in Context ('env/integration-diagnosis time, bug provenance, replanning/escalation classification') to eliminate the undefined term.
evidence: 'Tier-C blindspots' — used once in Success Criteria with no definition in Context, Approach, or Decomposition
Context names the blindspots directly: 'env/integration-diagnosis time, which change caused a bug, replanning/escalation classification' without using a Tier taxonomy
findings.finding: The term 'Tier-C blindspots' appears in Success Criteria without definition anywhere in the plan, while the same concept is described narratively in Context as specific blindspot categories — the terminology is inconsistent across sections.
findings.location: Success Criteria > item 3 ('Tier-C blindspots are captured passively')
scenarios: An implementer reads 'Tier-C' and either assumes an external taxonomy or invents their own scope boundary, potentially over- or under-implementing.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Tier-C blindspots' with 'the three agent-lifecycle blindspots identified in Context (env/integration-diagnosis time, bug provenance via caused_by, replanning/escalation classification via close --class)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly sequence ed13 before 555e (or vice versa) and note in 555e that `--caused-by` must be added alongside the already-present `--class` flag parsing.
evidence: 555e Files: 'src/rebar/_commands/transition.py — parse `--caused-by`' and 'src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — on bug close, best-effort auto-link `caused_by`; honor `--caused-by`'

ed13 Files: 'src/rebar/_commands/transition.py — parse a new `--class <value>` flag in `_parse_flags`' and 'src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — CRITICAL: the `_completion_precheck` guard at line ~121 also calls `txn.bug_close_reason_ok(reason)`; update it'
findings.finding: Children 555e and ed13 both modify the same files (`src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` and `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`) — 555e adds `--caused-by` flag parsing and ed13 adds `--class` flag parsing and the completion precheck guard — with no merge coordination or sequencing stated, creating a concrete code-level conflict.
findings.location: child 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 (Files section) and child ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (Files section)
scenarios: Two engineers implement 555e and ed13 in parallel; both edit `_parse_flags` in `transition.py` and the precheck guard in `transition_close.py`; the merge conflict is non-trivial because both touch the same logical block.
findings.suggested_fix: Mark ed13 as a prerequisite of 555e in the ticket dependency graph, and have 555e's implementation instructions note that transition.py already has --class from ed13 when 555e is implemented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the canonical metric-registry keys (by name, lens, and derivation source) in the plan so AC2's integration test has a fixed non-trivial set to assert against.
evidence: 'an integration test asserts via `jq -e` that no registered metric key is absent'
'metric-registry + unavailable/label contract' in Command track — the registry contents themselves are not listed anywhere in the plan
The plan names three lenses (agent-process, gate-economics, code-health) but does not enumerate the individual metric keys within each lens
findings.finding: The metric registry — the exact set of keys that 'every registered metric key' in AC2's integration test refers to — is never enumerated in the plan. An executor who defines a trivially small or wrong registry will pass the test vacuously, and two independent implementers would produce incompatible registries.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; Decomposition — Command track
scenarios: Executor A implements a 5-key registry; Executor B implements a 20-key registry; both pass AC2 trivially, producing incompatible outputs
AC2 integration test cannot be written without first defining the registry in code — the test is vacuous without a canonical key list
A reviewer cannot audit whether all blindspots named in Context are closed without knowing which metric keys map to each blindspot
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or list under the Command track section with columns: metric_key | lens | derivation_source | notes. At minimum include one key per named blindspot (env-diagnosis latency, bug-provenance, replanning-rate, coverage, complexity, clone, cycle-time, gate-pass-rate) so the registry is independently reproducible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] List the intended `--class` enum values (e.g., `regression | logic-error | integration-failure | spec-mismatch | other`) in the plan so the persisted schema and backfill mappings are unambiguous.
evidence: 'the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded `--class` enum'
No enum values (e.g., regression, logic-error, integration-failure, spec-mismatch) appear anywhere in the plan
Backfill track references 'historical bug --class from structural signals' but the structural mapping is undefined without knowing the enum
findings.finding: The `--class` enum values for the bug-close classification are never listed. AC4 asserts 'a bounded `--class` enum' and that it is 'persisted on the STATUS event', but an executor must invent the enum members with no guidance, making the deliverable underspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; Instrumentation track ('the bug close --class enum (persisted, replacing the discarded reason)')
scenarios: Two executors pick different enum cardinalities and labels, making historical data incompatible
The backfill adapter cannot map structural signals to classes without a defined enum
A reviewer cannot verify the enum is 'bounded' in any meaningful sense
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line like: 'Initial enum values: `regression`, `logic-error`, `integration-failure`, `spec-mismatch`, `other` (extensible; new values are additive).' under the Instrumentation track or AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the ERROR outcome trigger condition (e.g., non-zero exit from infra/setup phase, network timeout) and the minimal sidecar schema (fields: `outcome`, `phase`, `started_at`, `ended_at`, `error_code`) so gate error handlers can write it consistently.
evidence: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
'always stamped by trustworthy code (gate error handlers, git blame) rather than agent self-report'
No definition of ERROR vs FAIL vs PASS distinction in gate sidecars, nor schema for the ERROR record
findings.finding: The condition that triggers an `ERROR` outcome on gate sidecars is not specified. The plan states env/integration-diagnosis latency is captured 'via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars' but does not define what constitutes an error vs. a pass/fail, who writes the ERROR field, or what data (timestamp, error code, phase) is recorded.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Tier-C blindspots'; Approach — 'gate error handlers'
scenarios: Executor conflates a test-failure (FAIL) with an infra/env error (ERROR), polluting the diagnosis-time metric
Two gate implementations write ERROR in incompatible formats, breaking the reader in the Command track
The latency metric is undefined: start time? end time? which phase boundary?

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies `caused_by` is persisted and readable: e.g., `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[] | select(.rel=="caused_by")'` after a bug is closed with `--caused-by` or blame-auto-derivation fires.
evidence: Instrumentation track names: 'the caused_by link relation (additive); blame-based caused_by auto-derivation + --caused-by param + bug-template advisory'
AC2 verifies JSON output of registered metrics; AC3 verifies source/confidence segregation; AC4 verifies --class enum; none verify that caused_by is written or readable on a ticket
findings.finding: The 'caused_by' deliverable — link relation, blame-based auto-derivation, --caused-by param, and bug-template advisory — is described in the Instrumentation track but no AC verifies that the link relation is written, persisted, or queryable on a closed bug ticket.
findings.location: Decomposition > Instrumentation track; Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
scenarios: Implementer ships the metrics command and --class enum but defers or drops caused_by with no acceptance gate to catch it.
caused_by auto-derivation is broken or missing in the event store and nothing in the test suite catches it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A bug ticket closed after auto-derivation (or via `--caused-by`) carries a `caused_by` link relation: `rebar show <ticket> --output json | jq -e ".links[] | select(.rel==\"caused_by\")"` exits 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) to verify snapshot round-trip: write a snapshot, then query `rebar metrics` over a range that includes it and assert the value appears as authoritative (not unavailable) in the output.
evidence: Command track lists: 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader'

AC2 asserts 'exits 0 and emits JSON where every registered metric key is present' but does not require round-trip persistence: a purely in-memory derivation could pass AC2 without any snapshot ever being written.
findings.finding: The 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader' deliverable in the Command track has no covering AC — AC2 only tests CLI output format and unavailable states, not that snapshots are durably written and later read back.
findings.location: Decomposition > Command track ('going-forward snapshot writer/reader'); Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: The snapshot writer is never implemented; metrics that can only be computed at a point-in-time (e.g. complexity at a commit) are always reported as unavailable because no snapshot was written, yet AC2 passes because unavailable is a valid response.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC4 to verify removal of the old free-text requirement: e.g., assert that `rebar close <bug> --class bug` (no free-text) exits 0, confirming the field is no longer required.
evidence: AC4: 'the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded --class enum persisted on the STATUS event, verified by rebar show <closed-bug> --output json | jq -e .close_class'
The verify step (`jq -e '.close_class'`) only checks the new field exists; it does not check that omitting a free-text reason no longer causes an error, nor that old free-text is deprecated.
findings.finding: AC4 verifies only that the new `close_class` field is present (replacement), but does not verify that the old 'required free-text reason' is removed or no longer required — the migration is half-specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > AC4
scenarios: Both the old free-text reason field and the new --class enum coexist; friction actually increases because the agent must supply both; AC4 still passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'and `rebar close <ticket> --class bug` (without a free-text reason argument) exits 0, confirming the previously-required free-text is no longer required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Decomposition whether ERROR-outcome *emission* belongs to the Instrumentation track (alongside caused_by and --class) and ERROR-outcome *reading* belongs to the Command track, or confirm the Command track owns both and explain how gate error handlers are modified without an instrumentation child.
evidence: Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars' — framed as a passive capture/instrumentation concern
Decomposition Command track: 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path' — places the ERROR path in the reader/command layer, not Instrumentation
Decomposition Instrumentation track: lists only caused_by and --class enum; ERROR emission is absent
findings.finding: Success Criteria frames ERROR-outcome emission on gate sidecars as passive instrumentation, but Decomposition places 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path' in the Command track rather than the Instrumentation track, creating an ambiguity about where the emission work lives.
findings.location: Success Criteria > item 3 vs. Decomposition > Command track
scenarios: One implementer adds ERROR-outcome emission to gate error handlers (Instrumentation, as SC implies); another treats it as purely a reader concern (Command track); both assume the other covered the emission side, and it is never emitted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit rollback/undo plan for the backfill operations: at minimum, require a dry-run mode and a pre-backfill snapshot (or reversible write strategy such as a separate backfill branch), and state the procedure for bulk-removing or correcting backfilled records if the heuristics prove inaccurate.
evidence: "Backfill (one-time, labeled): `caused_by` over existing bugs; historical bug `--class` from structural signals"
"one-time" implies irreversibility
The plan says backfilled values are "labeled with `source`+`confidence` and segregated" — this is a read-time mitigation, not a write-time rollback
No mention of a dry-run mode, snapshot-before-backfill, or undo script for the backfill operations
findings.finding: The backfill track writes new fields (`caused_by` links, `close_class` values) to existing event-store records in a one-time operation described as irreversible, but the plan provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan for the backfill itself — only labeling/segregation of the results.
findings.location: Decomposition — Backfill track; Success Criteria — 'backfilled/classified values are labeled with source+confidence'
scenarios: Backfill misclassifies a batch of bugs (e.g., blame points to a formatter commit); the event store now contains erroneous `caused_by` links with no documented way to remove or correct them in bulk.
A partial backfill failure mid-run leaves the event store in an inconsistent state with no recovery procedure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--dry-run` flag to the backfill commands that prints what would be written without touching the store. Before any live backfill run, checkpoint the affected branch (e.g., `git stash` or a tagged commit). Document a `rebar backfill --undo <run-id>` or equivalent that deletes records written in a specific backfill run (feasible if each backfill write is stamped with a run ID).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where the `--class` enum values are defined (e.g., a config file, schema file, or registry entry) and confirm that adding a new class requires only a config/data change, not a code change.
evidence: 'the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded `--class` enum'
'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text reason'
No mention of where the enum is defined, how it is versioned, or whether values can be added via configuration rather than code change.
findings.finding: The bug-close `--class` enum encodes domain-specific taxonomy (bug categories) that is highly likely to evolve as new classification needs emerge, but the plan specifies no mechanism for extending or configuring the enum without a code change — it is treated as a bounded, fixed set.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded `--class` enum persisted on the STATUS event' / Decomposition — Instrumentation track: 'the bug close `--class` enum (persisted, replacing the discarded reason)'
scenarios: A team adopts rebar and discovers a bug class (e.g., 'data-corruption' or 'regression-from-dependency-upgrade') not in the initial enum; adding it requires a code change and release rather than a config/schema update, contradicting the plan's goal of zero-friction instrumentation.
findings.suggested_fix: Define the allowed `--class` values in a versioned config file (e.g., `.rebar/metric-config.yml`) or the rebar project config, so new categories can be added by editing config rather than patching code. Document the extension point in the child ticket for the Instrumentation track.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to producing an ADR (or AGENTS.md / design-doc section) covering the layered-hybrid metric derivation architecture, the metric registry `unavailable`/`source`/`confidence` contract, and the backfill-adapter isolation boundary before the Command track child ticket is closed.
evidence: 'Derive-structurally over the durable event store … add the smallest possible passive emission … harness/CI-specific backfills are isolated optional adapters'
'metric-registry + unavailable/label contract; event-derivation readers; git-derivation readers; gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path; going-forward snapshot writer/reader'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
The rubric (T5e documentation) flags MAJOR severity when a new pipeline stage or cross-component coupling has no doc/ADR.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel layered-hybrid architectural pattern (structural event-sourced derivation + passive emission + labeled backfill adapters), a metric registry contract with a defined `unavailable`/`source`/`confidence` schema, and a new pipeline stage — but does not commit to capturing any of these decisions in an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design doc.
findings.location: Approach section — 'the layered-hybrid design that dev-analytics (DevLake, Swarmia) and agent-observability practice converge on' / Decomposition — Command track: 'metric-registry + unavailable/label contract'
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new metric source and inadvertently bypasses the `source`+`confidence` labeling contract (no ADR documents it as mandatory); the segregation invariant silently breaks and backfill-classified values pollute authoritative series — the unit test catches it only after the fact, not at design time.

A new team member onboarding to rebar cannot find documentation explaining why the backfill adapters are isolated from the core command, leading them to couple a new adapter directly to the command internals.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Decomposition item or AC entry: 'An ADR is merged that documents (a) the metric registry contract and unavailable-state schema, (b) the backfill-adapter isolation boundary and why the core command must not depend on adapters, and (c) the source+confidence segregation invariant.' Reference the ADR in the relevant child tickets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify all consumers of the bug-close free-text `reason` field (formatters, reports, scripts) and specify whether the field is removed, aliased, or kept alongside `close_class` to preserve back-compatibility.
evidence: AC4: 'the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded --class enum persisted on the STATUS event'
Approach states changes are 'additive and back-compatible (old records stay valid; readers tolerate absence)' — but the free-text reason is being actively replaced, not merely supplemented.
No section names callers/consumers of the old reason field or describes a migration/compatibility shim.
findings.finding: The plan changes existing bug-close behavior (replacing the validated free-text `reason` with a persisted `--class` enum) but does not acknowledge or address existing callers — scripts, CI pipelines, or consumers — that may currently parse or display the old free-text reason field.
findings.location: Success Criteria – AC4; Instrumentation track – 'bug close --class enum (persisted, replacing the discarded reason)'
scenarios: A CI report, a `rebar show` formatter, or a downstream script that reads `.reason` from the STATUS event stops working after the change.
The 'back-compatible' claim in Approach is contradicted by actively replacing (not deprecating) the free-text field.
findings.suggested_fix: Either retain `reason` as an optional deprecated field populated from `close_class.label` for back-compat, or enumerate known consumers in the plan and confirm each is updated in the same epic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing annotation (or a thin spike child) to the Command track that validates event-derivation reader output against the registry contract BEFORE building the CLI and snapshot writer, so the riskiest assumption is evidence-gated early.
evidence: Plan lists all seven Command-track children as peers with no stated sequence: 'metric-registry + unavailable/label contract; event-derivation readers; git-derivation readers; gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path; going-forward snapshot writer/reader; the rebar metrics CLI'
No spike or evidence-gated checkpoint child is present that proves event-derivation produces usable data before the CLI and registry are built
snowwhite-mysterious-macropod (CLI) implicitly depends on all other Command-track children but is listed as a sibling
findings.finding: The seven Command-track children (registry, three readers, gate-sidecar reader, snapshot writer, CLI) carry implicit ordering dependencies but no explicit sequencing or thin-slice MVP checkpoint; the riskiest assumption — that event-derivation readers can extract meaningful metrics from the live event store — is not validated before the full CLI scaffold is built, creating big-bang integration risk across the track.
findings.location: Decomposition section — Command track (illadvised, bonelike, exciting, paralyzed, cinnamoned, cyberpunk, snowwhite)
scenarios: Team builds registry and CLI scaffold in parallel with event-derivation readers; when readers are integrated, schema mismatches or sparse data force rework of the registry contract and CLI rendering logic simultaneously
Event store turns out to lack sufficient signal density for meaningful trends; this is discovered only after the full CLI is integrated, too late to adjust scope cheaply
findings.suggested_fix: Nominate bonelike-facile-inganue (event-derivation readers) + illadvised-sophomoric-jellyfish (registry contract) as a gated Phase 1 whose output (a JSON fixture of raw derived metrics) is reviewed before snowwhite and cyberpunk are started; add a one-line sequencing note to the Decomposition section stating this order.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the Instrumentation child that the `bug_close_reason_ok` guard in `transition_core` (txn.py) and the `_completion_precheck` (transition_close.py) must be updated to accept a valid `--class` enum value as a substitute for the old `--reason` prefix check, so bug closes using `--class` do not fail the legacy reason guard.
evidence: src/rebar/_commands/txn.py:150: `def bug_close_reason_ok(reason: str) -> bool:` — raises if reason is empty or doesn't start with 'Fixed'/'Escalat'
src/rebar/_commands/txn.py:221-232: `if target_status == 'closed' and ticket_type == 'bug' and not from_idea:` raises CommandError if reason absent or fails the predicate
src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:121: `if ticket_type == 'bug' and not txn.bug_close_reason_ok(reason):` raises before LLM call
Plan text states 'replaced by a bounded --class enum' but names no explicit removal of these two guard sites
findings.finding: The plan replaces the required free-text `--reason` for bug closes with a bounded `--class` enum, but the existing codebase has two hardcoded guards — `txn.bug_close_reason_ok()` called in both `transition_core` and `_completion_precheck` — that raise `CommandError` when `--reason` is absent or doesn't start with 'Fixed:'/'Escalated to user:'. If these guards are not explicitly removed or conditioned on `--class`, every bug close using only `--class` (no `--reason`) will still fail. The plan's AC ('required free-text reason is replaced') implies the guard is gone, but the implementation path for removing it is unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 4: 'Bug-close friction does not increase: the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded --class enum persisted on the STATUS event'
scenarios: Implementation adds `--class` enum to STATUS event data but omits updating the reason guards; every `rebar transition <bug> closed --class=fixed` without `--reason` returns exit 1 with 'must start with Fixed:', breaking the very friction-reduction goal.
A migration period where both `--reason` and `--class` are accepted risks the guards drifting again in the future.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Instrumentation child, add an AC: 'The `bug_close_reason_ok` check in `txn.transition_core` and `transition_close._completion_precheck` is gated on `close_class` being set when `--class` replaces `--reason`; a unit test asserts that `transition <bug> closed --class=fixed` exits 0 without `--reason`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the Command track spec for the going-forward snapshot writer/reader: specify whether snapshots are committed to the tickets branch (like other events), stored in a dedicated branch, or kept in a local-only cache — and confirm that the chosen location survives `git clone` and CI ephemeral environments.
evidence: Plan text: 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader' — no storage medium, path, or git-commit policy specified
Approach section: 'Derive-structurally over the durable event store' implies git-committed persistence, but this component is new and not derived from existing events
The tickets branch is the established durable store; the snapshot store in _snapshot/repo_snapshot.py is ephemeral (tempdir-based, content-addressed by SHA)
findings.finding: The 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader' component (Command track) has no specified storage location or durability contract. The rest of the plan is careful to anchor everything to the durable, git-committed event store (tickets branch), but this component — which will persist code-health snapshots that have no event-store source — is described only by name. If it stores data outside the git-committed store (e.g. a local flat file, /tmp, or an in-process cache), it won't survive clone operations, branch switches, or CI ephemeral environments, breaking the 'complete history' promise for complexity/clone/coverage metrics.
findings.location: Decomposition, Command track: 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader'
scenarios: An agent runs on a fresh CI clone and `rebar metrics --since 2026-01-01` reports all complexity/clone snapshots as `unavailable` because the snapshot store didn't persist alongside the tickets branch.
Two team members run `rebar metrics` and see different historical series because each has local-only snapshot data.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a batching/throttling strategy for the Backfill track (e.g., process N events per run with a watermark cursor), so no single execution is unbounded.
evidence: 'Backfill (one-time, labeled): `caused_by` over existing bugs; historical bug `--class` from structural signals; complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
No mention of batch size, rate limiting, chunking, or progress cursor anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The Backfill track ('one-time, labeled') covering caused_by over existing bugs, historical bug --class, and complexity/clone snapshots specifies no batching or throttling strategy — a large event store could make this a single blocking operation.
findings.location: Decomposition — Backfill track
scenarios: A rebar project with hundreds of bug-close events and thousands of commits triggers a single-pass blame + rewrite operation that locks or corrupts the event store mid-run.
A runner with a timeout kills the process mid-backfill; with no batching checkpoint, it must restart from scratch or leaves data half-applied.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a cursor file (e.g., `.rebar/backfill-cursor.json`) recording the last processed ticket ID; each run processes a fixed batch (e.g., 50 tickets) and updates the cursor, making runs bounded and naturally resumable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an idempotency contract for each backfill operation (e.g., 'skip if field already present with source=structural', or use a cursor watermark), so partial runs can be safely re-executed.
evidence: 'Backfill (one-time, labeled)' — 'one-time' implies single-shot, not re-runnable.
No checkpoint, cursor, or idempotency guard is described for any of the three backfill operations.
AC3: 'a `source=backfill_classified` value is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series' — addresses read-time segregation, not write-time idempotency.
findings.finding: The Backfill track is described as 'one-time' with no idempotency or resumability guarantee — if a backfill run is interrupted partway through, re-running it may double-apply labels or skip already-processed records.
findings.location: Decomposition — Backfill track
scenarios: Process is killed after backfilling 200 of 500 bugs; re-run re-processes the first 200, adding duplicate caused_by entries or overwriting already-correct close_class values.
A bug already assigned close_class=regression is overwritten by the structural-signal classifier on re-run, silently degrading data quality.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an expand-contract sequence for the `--class` enum change: (1) expand — persist close_class as an optional additional field on STATUS events while still accepting free-text; (2) backfill existing events; (3) contract — make close_class required and drop free-text; and specify how concurrent writes during the backfill window are handled.
evidence: 'the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded `--class` enum persisted on the STATUS event'
'replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text reason'
'old records stay valid; readers tolerate absence' — addresses forward-read safety but not concurrent writes during cutover.
No dual-write window, feature flag, or phased cutover described.
findings.finding: The bug close `--class` enum replaces the free-text reason on STATUS events without any expand-contract phasing — new STATUS events written during and after the migration cutover will differ structurally from pre-existing ones, and the plan does not address how concurrent writes during the backfill window are handled.
findings.location: Decomposition — Instrumentation track; Success Criteria SC3; Acceptance Criteria AC4
scenarios: An agent closes a bug while the historical --class backfill is mid-run: the new STATUS event uses the new enum format, but the backfill script, written against the old format, fails to recognise it or overwrites the fresh close_class.
A rollback of the --class feature after partial deployment leaves a mix of STATUS events with and without close_class, with no described remediation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify the post-rollback schema state for each persisted-format change (`caused_by`, `close_class`, snapshot files) and provide a back-out path or acknowledge DATA LOSS RISK where compensating-forward is the only safe option.
evidence: 'old records stay valid; readers tolerate absence' — describes tolerance of absent fields, not post-rollback schema state.
No rollback procedure, compensating-forward path, or explicit acknowledgement of DATA LOSS RISK on partial backfill failure is described anywhere in the plan.
The `caused_by` link and close_class are written into the event store (durable, append-only); once written, removing them is itself a destructive mutation.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'old records stay valid; readers tolerate absence' but never specifies the schema state AFTER a rollback of the --class or caused_by changes — a mixed state (some events with the new fields, some without) is possible after partial deployment and rollback, and the plan provides no back-out path or DATA LOSS RISK acknowledgement.
findings.location: Approach paragraph; Decomposition — Instrumentation and Backfill tracks
scenarios: Backfill writes caused_by links to 150 of 300 bugs and then fails; rollback of the Instrumentation track code leaves 150 bugs with links pointing to commits that future tooling no longer knows to interpret, with no documented remediation.

The --class backfill writes close_class to historical STATUS events; if the metrics command is then rolled back, those annotated events persist forever in the event store in a state the rolled-back reader was never designed to handle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the schema tag and field shape for the `ERROR` outcome on gate sidecars (e.g., a new `completion_verifier_error_v1` tag on `COMPLETION_VERDICT`, or an `outcome` discriminator field), confirm it is registered in `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` and `_store/event_append.EVENT_TYPES`, and verify the metrics command reader can distinguish ERROR from FAIL records.
evidence: Plan: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
Existing COMPLETION_VERDICT schema tags: `completion_verifier_fail_v1` and `completion_verifier_pass_v1` — no ERROR tag (src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py lines 29, 34)
Existing REVIEW_RESULT event type only carries plan-review PASS/FAIL verdicts (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py)
Partially-verified evidence class: the sidecar mechanism is confirmed present; the ERROR outcome surface is a new schema field not present today
findings.finding: The plan introduces an `ERROR` outcome on 'existing gate sidecars' to capture env/integration-diagnosis time, but neither the `REVIEW_RESULT` nor the `COMPLETION_VERDICT` sidecar schemas currently have an `ERROR` verdict/outcome value. The current schemas only carry `PASS`/`FAIL` verdicts. Adding `ERROR` as a new outcome on an existing sidecar event type is a new schema surface; the plan does not specify whether this extends the existing `SCHEMA`/`SCHEMA_PASS` tags, introduces a new schema tag (e.g. `completion_verifier_error_v1`), or is a new field alongside `verdict`. Without this, the metrics command reader cannot reliably distinguish `ERROR` sidecars from `FAIL` sidecars, and an `fsck` check would flag the new shape as unknown.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Tier-C blindspots: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
scenarios: The metrics command reads COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars and tries to separate ERROR records from FAIL records; without a distinct schema tag, the reader has no stable discriminator.
An `fsck` run after the new ERROR sidecars are emitted warns 'newer than me' if the schema tag is not registered in `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (src/rebar/_store/event_append.py lines 85–98).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `--class` enum vocabulary (all allowed values with semantics) in the plan — e.g., `fixed`, `wont_fix`, `duplicate`, `escalated`, `invalid` — so both the CLI and the metrics reader share the same authoritative list.
evidence: Plan AC4: 'the required free-text reason is replaced by a bounded `--class` enum persisted on the STATUS event'
Plan AC4: 'rebar show <closed-bug> --output json | jq -e ".close_class"'
No enum members (e.g., `fixed`, `wont_fix`, `duplicate`, `escalated`) are listed anywhere in the plan
src/rebar/_commands/txn.py:150 shows current implementation accepts 'Fixed:' / 'Escalated to user:' prefixed free text — the replacement enum's full value set is completely absent from the plan
findings.finding: The `--class` enum introduced to replace the bug-close free-text reason is referenced in the Success Criteria, the Instrumentation track, and AC4, but its value vocabulary is never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4; Success Criteria ('bounded `--class` enum')
scenarios: An executor implementing the `--class` param has no authoritative list of allowed values — they will invent their own taxonomy, making the AC4 assertion unverifiable against a spec.
The jq assertion `jq -e '.close_class'` only checks key presence, not value validity; without a defined vocabulary, any string passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or list of enum members to the Instrumentation track section, alongside the AC4 description. Example members: `fixed` (root cause addressed in code), `escalated` (passed upstream), `wont_fix` (accepted risk), `duplicate` (covered by another ticket), `invalid` (not a real bug). Document which old `Fixed:`/`Escalated to user:` prefix maps to which enum value for backward-compat backfill.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `caused_by` relation semantics in the Instrumentation track: define directionality (which ticket is source, which is target), reciprocal/unidirectional treatment (mirrors `discovered_from` pattern or `relates_to`), and whether it is added to `MANAGED_REF_KINDS` for sync propagation.
evidence: Plan: 'the `caused_by` link relation (additive)'
Plan: 'blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation + `--caused-by` param + bug-template advisory'
Existing relation vocabulary confirmed in src/rebar/_cli/help/link.txt: 'blocks | depends_on | relates_to | duplicates | supersedes | discovered_from' — `caused_by` is absent
src/rebar/reducer/_managed_refs.py:50: MANAGED_REF_KINDS = ('parent', 'blocks', 'depends_on', 'relates_to') — does not include `caused_by`
src/rebar/_commands/unlink.py:28: usage string lists all 6 current relations with 'discovered_from are directional (no reciprocal link)' — no protocol for `caused_by` directionality specified in plan
findings.finding: The `caused_by` link relation is introduced as a new additive relation, but its directionality semantics, reciprocal/unidirectional treatment, and whether it is added to `MANAGED_REF_KINDS` are never specified in the plan.
findings.location: Decomposition — Instrumentation track ('the `caused_by` link relation (additive)')
scenarios: An executor adding `caused_by` to the LINK event vocabulary must decide: is it `bug caused_by commit` or `commit caused_by bug`? Is it managed (sync-propagated) or not? Does it need a reciprocal? None of this is answerable from the plan.
The metrics reader that derives 'bug provenance' from `caused_by` links needs a stable, defined semantics to traverse the relation correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `ERROR` outcome schema for gate sidecars: specify which event type carries it, what fields distinguish it from FAIL (e.g., an `outcome` enum with values `PASS`/`FAIL`/`ERROR`), what diagnostic fields it carries (e.g., `error_class`, `duration_ms`), and which gate error handlers are responsible for emitting it.
evidence: Plan Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py:29: SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'; SCHEMA_PASS = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1' — no ERROR schema defined
Plan Approach: 'add the smallest possible passive emission only where a signal genuinely leaves no trace, always stamped by trustworthy code (gate error handlers, git blame)'
No payload shape, event type, or `outcome` field vocabulary is specified for the ERROR path anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces an `ERROR` outcome on existing gate sidecars to capture env/integration-diagnosis time, but no payload schema, field definitions, or protocol for which gate error handlers emit `ERROR` are provided — the existing sidecar schemas (`completion_verifier_fail_v1`, `completion_verifier_pass_v1`) have no such outcome.
findings.location: Success Criteria ('env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars')
scenarios: An executor implementing the ERROR emission path has no schema to emit against — they will invent fields, making the gate-sidecar reader in the Command track unable to parse ERROR records.
The metrics reader that computes 'env/integration-diagnosis time' cannot distinguish ERROR from FAIL without a defined `outcome` field and its allowed values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `unavailable` sub-schema in the plan: specify the allowed `reason` vocabulary and the type/format of `accruing_since` (e.g., ISO-8601 date string, or null if the signal has never been observed).
evidence: Plan AC2: 'emits JSON where every registered metric key is present with either a value or {"unavailable":{"reason","accruing_since"}}'
No definition of `reason` values (e.g., 'no_data', 'signal_not_instrumented', 'date_range_too_early') anywhere in the plan
No definition of `accruing_since` type (ISO date string? HLC timestamp? first-event date?) anywhere in the plan
The integration test is defined to assert via `jq -e` that no registered metric key is absent — but the test cannot validate `unavailable` shape without a spec
findings.finding: The `unavailable` sub-object referenced in AC2's integration-test assertion (`{"unavailable":{"reason","accruing_since"}}`) is never schema-specified: the allowed vocabulary for `reason` and the type/semantics of `accruing_since` are undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC2
scenarios: Two Command-track child implementers produce different `unavailable` shapes; the jq assertion passes for key presence but the downstream consumer (e.g., a dashboard) cannot parse the struct.
A metric that has no data uses `accruing_since: null` while another uses `accruing_since: '2026-01-01'` — without a spec, both are valid and the consumer cannot distinguish.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Command track section: `unavailable.reason` enum values: `no_history` (signal exists but no events in range), `not_instrumented` (passive capture not yet deployed), `insufficient_data` (fewer than N observations). `accruing_since`: ISO-8601 date of the first observed event for this signal, or `null` if none.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `source` enum vocabulary (all values, not just `backfill_classified`) and the `confidence` field type/scale in the plan, co-located with AC3.
evidence: Plan Success Criteria: 'backfilled/classified values are labeled with `source`+`confidence` and segregated from authoritative structural signals'
Plan AC3: 'a `source=backfill_classified` value is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series — a unit test asserts the segregation'
Only one `source` value is explicitly named (`backfill_classified`); values for authoritative/derived/inferred signals are never listed
The `confidence` field type is never defined: boolean? float 0–1? enum (high/medium/low)? This is load-bearing for the unit test in AC3.
findings.finding: The `source` and `confidence` fields required on every metric entry (AC3, Success Criteria) have an undefined vocabulary: only `source=backfill_classified` is named; all other `source` values and the type/scale of `confidence` are unspecified.
findings.location: Success Criteria ('backfilled/classified values are labeled with `source`+`confidence`'); Acceptance Criteria AC3
scenarios: The unit test in AC3 checks that `source=backfill_classified` is segregated, but without the full source vocabulary, a reviewer cannot verify that all non-authoritative sources are correctly classified.
The metrics reader aggregates `confidence` values but has no defined scale — one implementer uses 0.0–1.0, another uses 'high'/'medium'/'low', making aggregation semantics undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: Example: `source` enum: `event_derived` (from reducer/event store), `git_derived` (from blame/log), `gate_sidecar` (from REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT), `snapshot_computed` (from CI snapshots), `backfill_classified` (retrospective classification), `backfill_inferred` (inferred from structural signals). `confidence`: float 0.0–1.0 (1.0 = authoritative structural signal, <1.0 = inferred/backfilled).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 555e as an explicit prerequisite/dependency of 2f8e in the ticket dependency graph.
evidence: 2f8e Approach: 'Reuse the same conservative single-culprit resolver as the live auto-derivation' and 'scripts/backfill_caused_by.py — new: iterate closed bugs, reuse `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`'
555e Files: '`src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — new: conservative single-culprit blame -> commit -> ticket resolver'
No dependency link stated between 2f8e and 555e
findings.finding: Child 2f8e has an undeclared hard implementation dependency on child 555e: 2f8e's backfill script imports `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`, which is created by 555e. If 555e is not implemented first, 2f8e's script cannot run, but no ordering constraint is stated in either child.
findings.location: child 2f8e-0f7e-6fea-4825 (Files and Approach sections)
scenarios: 2f8e is assigned and implemented before 555e; the backfill script import of `rebar.metrics.blame` raises ImportError at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: In 2f8e's ticket, add a `depends_on` link to 555e, and note in the Files section that `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` is provided by 555e.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an append-safety protocol for `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (e.g. atomic rename, file lock, or serialize all writers through a single CI step); document this in 3275 as the owner of the write API.
evidence: 3275 Files: '`reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — new committed artifact (one snapshot per line)'
1f77 Files: '`.github/workflows/test.yml` — append the current coverage % to the snapshot store on each run'
c595 Files: 'scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py — ... append snapshots via `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`'
No locking or serialization protocol mentioned in any child
findings.finding: `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is written by three independent children (3275 snapshot writer, 1f77 GHA adapter, c595 complexity/clone backfill) with no append-safety or file-locking protocol defined, risking line interleaving or corruption when multiple writers run concurrently (e.g. parallel CI steps).
findings.location: child 3275-b164-2e9b-4e66 (Files section), child 1f77-f8c0-bfae-4a79 (Files section), child c595-9348-d4f2-4529 (Files section)
scenarios: Two CI jobs run simultaneously (e.g. a push and a PR); both append to metrics-snapshots.ndjson at the same time; the NDJSON file contains interleaved partial lines that the reader must skip (per 3275's 'malformed line is skipped' AC) — silently losing data.
findings.suggested_fix: In `snapshot.py` (3275), implement append using an OS-level file lock (e.g. `fcntl.flock`) or atomic write-to-temp-then-rename to make concurrent appends safe; document the protocol for all consumers.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
